You are an expert in OpenFOAM. Generate an Allrun script based on the provided details.Available commands with descriptions: <command>blockMesh</command><help_text>
Usage: blockMesh [OPTIONS]
options:
  -blockTopology    write block edges and centres as .obj files
  -case <dir>       specify alternate case directory, default is the cwd
  -dict <file>      read control dictionary from specified location
  -fileHandler <handler>
                    override the fileHandler
  -libs '("lib1.so" ... "libN.so")'
                    pre-load libraries
  -noClean          keep the existing files in the polyMesh
  -noFunctionObjects
                    do not execute functionObjects
  -region <name>    specify alternative mesh region
  -srcDoc           display source code in browser
  -doc              display application documentation in browser
  -help             print the usage

Block description

  For a given block, the correspondence between the ordering of
  vertex labels and face labels is shown below.
  For vertex numbering in the sequence 0 to 7 (block, centre):
    faces 0 (f0) and 1 are left and right, respectively;
    faces 2 and 3 are front and back; 
    and faces 4 and 5 are bottom and top::

                 7 ---- 6
            f5   |\     |\   f3
            |    | 4 ---- 5   \
            |    3 |--- 2 |    \
            |     \|     \|    f2
            f4     0 ---- 1

       Z         f0 ----- f1
       |  Y
       | /
       O --- X


Using: OpenFOAM-10 (see https://openfoam.org)
Build: 10-c4cf895ad8fa

</help_text>

  -srcDoc           display source code in browser
  -doc              display application documentation in browser
  -help             print the usage

Using: OpenFOAM-10 (see https://openfoam.org)

{

}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

scale   1;

vertices
(
    (0    0    0)        // 0
    (0.02 0    0)        // 1
    (0.02 0.02 0)        // 2
    (0    0.02 0)        // 3

    (0.02 0.02 0.001)    // 6
    (0    0.02 0.001)    // 7
);

blocks
(
    hex (0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7) (40 40 1) simpleGrading (1 1 1)
);

edges
(
);

boundary
(
    fuel
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (0 1 5 4)
        );
    }
    air
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (3 7 6 2)
        );
    }
    outlet
    {
        type patch;
        faces
        (
            (1 2 6 5)
            (0 4 7 3)
        );
    }
    frontAndBack
    {

        faces
        (
            (0 3 2 1)
            (4 5 6 7)
        );
    }
);

mergePatchPairs
(
);

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='controlDict', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    location    "system";
    object      controlDict;
}

application     reactingFoam;

startFrom       startTime;

startTime       0;

stopAt          endTime;

endTime         0.5;

deltaT          1e-6;

writeControl    adjustableRunTime;

writeInterval   0.05;

purgeWrite      0;

writeFormat     ascii;

writePrecision  8;

writeCompression off;

timeFormat      general;

timePrecision   6;

runTimeModifiable true;

adjustTimeStep  yes;

maxCo           0.4;

maxDeltaT       1e-4;'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSchemes', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSchemes;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

ddtSchemes
{
    default         Euler;
}

gradSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear;
}

divSchemes
{
    default         none;

    div(phi,U)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,K)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,h)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,k)      Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,epsilon) Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,Yi_h)   Gauss upwind;
    div(phi,K)      Gauss linear;
    div(((rho*nuEff)*dev2(T(grad(U))))) Gauss linear;
}

laplacianSchemes
{
    default         Gauss linear corrected;
}

interpolationSchemes
{
    default         linear;
}

snGradSchemes
{
    default         corrected;
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='fvSolution', folder_name='system', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      fvSolution;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

solvers
{
    "rho.*"
    {
        solver          diagonal;
    }

    p
    {
        solver          PCG;
        preconditioner  DIC;
        tolerance       1e-06;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    pFinal
    {
        $p;
        relTol          0;
    }

    "(U|h|k|epsilon)"
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-06;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(U|h|k|epsilon)Final"
    {
        $U;
        relTol          0;
    }

    "(Yi)"
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-08;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(Yi)Final"
    {
        $Yi;
        relTol          0;
    }
}

PIMPLE
{
    momentumPredictor   yes;
    nOuterCorrectors    1;
    nCorrectors         2;
    nNonOrthogonalCorrectors 0;
}

relaxationFactors
{
    equations
    {
        ".*"            1;
    }
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='chemistryProperties', folder_name='constant', content='FoamFile
{
    version     2.0;
    format      ascii;
    class       dictionary;
    object      chemistryProperties;
}
// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

chemistryType
{
    solver          ode;
    method          standard;
}

chemistry       on;

initialChemicalTimeStep 1e-07;

odeCoeffs
{
    solver          seulex;
    absTol          1e-12;
    relTol          1e-01;
}

// ************************************************************************* //'), FoamfilePydantic(file_name='combustionProperties', folder_name='constant', content='FoamFile

// * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * * //

solvers
{
    "rho.*"
    {
        solver          diagonal;
    }

    p
    {
        solver          PCG;
        preconditioner  DIC;
        tolerance       1e-06;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    pFinal
    {
        $p;
        relTol          0;
    }

    "(U|h|k|epsilon)"
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-06;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(U|h|k|epsilon)Final"
    {
        $U;
        relTol          0;
    }

    "(Yi)"
    {
        solver          PBiCGStab;
        preconditioner  DILU;
        tolerance       1e-08;
        relTol          0.1;
    }

    "(Yi)Final"
    {
        $Yi;
        relTol          0;
    }
}

PIMPLE
{
    momentumPredictor   yes;
    nOuterCorrectors    1;
    nCorrectors         2;
    nNonOrthogonalCorrectors 0;
}

relaxationFactors
{
    equations
    {
        ".*"            1;
    }
}

<review_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how thermophysical properties and chemistry are handled. The supported mixture types are `pureMixture`, `coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture`, `multiComponentMixture`, and `valueMultiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, OpenFOAM 10 has moved away from the old `hePsiThermo`/`chemicalSystem` framework toward a more unified `fluidReactionThermo` approach.

## Root Cause

The `physicalProperties` file specifies:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But OpenFOAM 10 does not recognize `reactingMixture`. Furthermore, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also uses `reactingMixture` and references `foamChemistryReader`, which is also outdated for OF10.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Change `mixture` type in `constant/physicalProperties`

In the `physicalProperties` file, change:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```
to:
```
mixture         multiComponentMixture;
```

Also update the `thermoType` block to remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries since those are not needed when using `multiComponentMixture` with species listed directly in the file. The `physicalProperties` should look like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### 2. Update `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Similarly, update `thermo.compressibleGas` to use `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

Remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries, and add the same `specie` sub-dictionary with molecular weights as shown above.

### 3. Verify `reactions` file compatibility

The `reactions` file should remain largely unchanged, but ensure the reaction is properly formatted. In OpenFOAM 10, the reaction format may need slight adjustment. Consider adding a `#include` directive or ensuring the reaction syntax follows the current format. Also verify that the `chemistryProperties` file's `chemistryType` settings are compatible — the `ode` solver with `standard` method should work fine.

### Summary

The primary fix is replacing `reactingMixture` with `multiComponentMixture` throughout the thermophysical property files (`physicalProperties` and `thermo.compressibleGas`), while removing the deprecated `chemistryReader`/`foamChemistry*` entries that are no longer used in OpenFOAM 10's fluid reaction thermo framework. This aligns with the OpenFOAM 10-supported mixture types and resolves the compilation error.</review_analysis>

valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how thermophysical properties and chemistry are handled. The supported mixture types are `pureMixture`, `coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture`, `multiComponentMixture`, and `valueMultiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, OpenFOAM 10 has moved away from the old `hePsiThermo`/`chemicalSystem` framework toward a more unified `fluidReactionThermo` approach.

## Root Cause

The `physicalProperties` file specifies:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But OpenFOAM 10 does not recognize `reactingMixture`. Furthermore, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also uses `reactingMixture` and references `foamChemistryReader`, which is also outdated for OF10.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Change `mixture` type in `constant/physicalProperties`

In the `physicalProperties` file, change:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```
to:
```
mixture         multiComponentMixture;
```

Also update the `thermoType` block to remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries since those are not needed when using `multiComponentMixture` with species listed directly in the file. The `physicalProperties` should look like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### 2. Update `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Similarly, update `thermo.compressibleGas` to use `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

Remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries, and add the same `specie` sub-dictionary with molecular weights as shown above.

### 3. Verify `reactions` file compatibility

The `reactions` file should remain largely unchanged, but ensure the reaction is properly formatted. In OpenFOAM 10, the reaction format may need slight adjustment. Consider adding a `#include` directive or ensuring the reaction syntax follows the current format. Also verify that the `chemistryProperties` file's `chemistryType` settings are compatible — the `ode` solver with `standard` method should work fine.

### Summary

The primary fix is replacing `reactingMixture` with `multiComponentMixture` throughout the thermophysical property files (`physicalProperties` and `thermo.compressibleGas`), while removing the deprecated `chemistryReader`/`foamChemistry*` entries that are no longer used in OpenFOAM 10's fluid reaction thermo framework. This aligns with the OpenFOAM 10-supported mixture types and resolves the compilation error.</reviewer_analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how thermophysical properties and chemistry are handled. The supported mixture types are `pureMixture`, `coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture`, `multiComponentMixture`, and `valueMultiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, OpenFOAM 10 has moved away from the old `hePsiThermo`/`chemicalSystem` framework toward a more unified `fluidReactionThermo` approach.

## Root Cause

The `physicalProperties` file specifies:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But OpenFOAM 10 does not recognize `reactingMixture`. Furthermore, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also uses `reactingMixture` and references `foamChemistryReader`, which is also outdated for OF10.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Change `mixture` type in `constant/physicalProperties`

In the `physicalProperties` file, change:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```
to:
```
mixture         multiComponentMixture;
```

Also update the `thermoType` block to remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries since those are not needed when using `multiComponentMixture` with species listed directly in the file. The `physicalProperties` should look like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}

<review_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means that OpenFOAM 10 expects a `species` entry inside the `physicalProperties` file when using `multiComponentMixture`. In OpenFOAM 10's `fluidReactionThermo` framework with `multiComponentMixture`, the species information (names, molecular weights, etc.) must be specified directly within `physicalProperties`, rather than being read from a separate `thermo.compressibleGas` file via `chemistryReader`.

Looking at the current setup:

1. **`physicalProperties`** has `multiComponentMixture` but only contains `specie`, `inertSpecie`, and `thermoType` — it's missing the top-level `species` keyword that `multiComponentMixture` requires.

2. **`thermo.compressibleGas`** still contains `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries, which are not needed and may cause issues since we're now handling species directly in `physicalProperties`.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Add `species` entry to `constant/physicalProperties`

Add the `species` keyword directly into `physicalProperties` with the species names matching those defined elsewhere in the case. It should contain all five species: `CH4`, `O2`, `N2`, `H2O`, `CO2`.

The updated `physicalProperties` should look like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### 2. Clean up `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Remove the following entries from `thermo.compressibleGas` since they are not needed when species are defined directly in `physicalProperties`:

- Remove the entire `chemistryReader` block
- Remove the `foamChemistryFile` entry
- Remove the `foamChemistryThermoFile` entry

Keep only the `thermoType` block with `multiComponentMixture` (which should already be set correctly), plus the `specie` sub-dictionary if present.

### Summary

The key fix is adding the `species` keyword to `physicalProperties` as required by OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` type. The species list must include all five species (CH4, O2, N2, H2O, CO2). Additionally, clean up `thermo.compressibleGas` by removing the deprecated `chemistryReader` and file reference entries that are incompatible with the direct-species-in-physical-properties approach used in OF10.</review_analysis>

<reviewer_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means that OpenFOAM 10 expects a `species` entry inside the `physicalProperties` file when using `multiComponentMixture`. In OpenFOAM 10's `fluidReactionThermo` framework with `multiComponentMixture`, the species information (names, molecular weights, etc.) must be specified directly within `physicalProperties`, rather than being read from a separate `thermo.compressibleGas` file via `chemistryReader`.

Looking at the current setup:

1. **`physicalProperties`** has `multiComponentMixture` but only contains `specie`, `inertSpecie`, and `thermoType` — it's missing the top-level `species` keyword that `multiComponentMixture` requires.

2. **`thermo.compressibleGas`** still contains `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries, which are not needed and may cause issues since we're now handling species directly in `physicalProperties`.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Add `species` entry to `constant/physicalProperties`

Add the `species` keyword directly into `physicalProperties` with the species names matching those defined elsewhere in the case. It should contain all five species: `CH4`, `O2`, `N2`, `H2O`, `CO2`.

The updated `physicalProperties` should look like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### 2. Clean up `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Remove the following entries from `thermo.compressibleGas` since they are not needed when species are defined directly in `physicalProperties`:

- Remove the entire `chemistryReader` block
- Remove the `foamChemistryFile` entry
- Remove the `foamChemistryThermoFile` entry

Keep only the `thermoType` block with `multiComponentMixture` (which should already be set correctly), plus the `specie` sub-dictionary if present.

### Summary

The key fix is adding the `species` keyword to `physicalProperties` as required by OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` type. The species list must include all five species (CH4, O2, N2, H2O, CO2). Additionally, clean up `thermo.compressibleGas` by removing the deprecated `chemistryReader` and file reference entries that are incompatible with the direct-species-in-physical-properties approach used in OF10.</reviewer_analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how thermophysical properties and chemistry are handled. The supported mixture types are `pureMixture`, `coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture`, `multiComponentMixture`, and `valueMultiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, OpenFOAM 10 has moved away from the old `hePsiThermo`/`chemicalSystem` framework toward a more unified `fluidReactionThermo` approach.

## Root Cause

The `physicalProperties` file specifies:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But OpenFOAM 10 does not recognize `reactingMixture`. Furthermore, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also uses `reactingMixture` and references `foamChemistryReader`, which is also outdated for OF10.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Change `mixture` type in `constant/physicalProperties`

In the `physicalProperties` file, change:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```
to:
```
mixture         multiComponentMixture;
```

Also update the `thermoType` block to remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries since those are not needed when using `multiComponentMixture` with species listed directly in the file. The `physicalProperties` should look like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### 2. Update `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Similarly, update `thermo.compressibleGas` to use `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

Remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries, and add the same `specie` sub-dictionary with molecular weights as shown above.

### 3. Verify `reactions` file compatibility

The `reactions` file should remain largely unchanged, but ensure the reaction is properly formatted. In OpenFOAM 10, the reaction format may need slight adjustment. Consider adding a `#include` directive or ensuring the reaction syntax follows the current format. Also verify that the `chemistryProperties` file's `chemistryType` settings are compatible — the `ode` solver with `standard` method should work fine.

### Summary

The primary fix is replacing `reactingMixture` with `multiComponentMixture` throughout the thermophysical property files (`physicalProperties` and `thermo.compressibleGas`), while removing the deprecated `chemistryReader`/`foamChemistry*` entries that are no longer used in OpenFOAM 10's fluid reaction thermo framework. This aligns with the OpenFOAM 10-supported mixture types and resolves the compilation error.

In OpenFOAM 10's `fluidReactionThermo` framework with `multiComponentMixture`, the species information (names, molecular weights, etc.) must be specified directly within `physicalProperties`, rather than being read from a separate `thermo.compressibleGas` file via `chemistryReader`.

Looking at the current setup:

1. **`physicalProperties`** has `multiComponentMixture` but only contains `specie`, `inertSpecie`, and `thermoType` — it's missing the top-level `species` keyword that `multiComponentMixture` requires.

2. **`thermo.compressibleGas`** still contains `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries, which are not needed and may cause issues since we're now handling species directly in `physicalProperties`.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Add `species` entry to `constant/physicalProperties`

<review_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means that OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` expects a `specie` sub-dictionary **inside** the `physicalProperties` file for each species, rather than having a single shared `specie` block at the top level. Looking at the history of attempts:

- **Attempt 1**: Fixed `reactingMixture` → `multiComponentMixture` (correct)
- **Attempt 2**: Added `species` list (correct) but the error changed to `keyword species is undefined` → then fixed that
- Now: The error is `keyword CH4 is undefined` — meaning the individual species' `specie` sub-dictionaries are missing from `physicalProperties`

In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, each species must have its own `specie` entry **directly in** `physicalProperties` (not just one global `specie` block). The solver looks up `specie` for each species individually, and since there's only one global `specie` block containing all five species, it fails to find the individual `specie` entry for `CH4`.

## Proposed Solution

**Modify `constant/physicalProperties`** to include individual `specie` sub-dictionaries for each species directly in the main dictionary (or accessible from it). There are two approaches:

### Approach A (Preferred): Individual specie entries for each species

Place each species' `specie` sub-dictionary directly in `physicalProperties` at the top level, e.g.:

```
specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
}
specie
{
    O2        31.998;
}
specie
{
    N2        28.014;
}
specie
{
    H2O       18.015;
}
specie
{
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### Approach B: Single combined specie block with all entries

Alternatively, keep a single `specie` block but ensure it contains **all five species** with their full molecular weight data (which it already does). Wait — looking at the current file, the `specie` block **already contains all five species**. So why is it failing?

Actually, re-reading the error carefully: it says `keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary "...physicalProperties"`. This suggests the solver is looking for a **top-level entry named exactly `CH4`**, not finding it in the `specie` sub-dictionary. 

In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the solver expects each species to have its thermodynamic data available at the top level of `physicalProperties`. The correct format is to have entries like:

```
CH4       12.011;
O2        31.998;
N2        28.014;
H2O       18.015;
CO2       44.01;
```

...as **flat entries** in `physicalProperties`, not nested inside a `specie { }` block.

### Fix

Remove the `specie { ... }` block from `physicalProperties` entirely, and place the five species with their molecular weights as **direct top-level entries**:

```
inertSpecie N2;

CH4       12.011;
O2        31.998;
N2        28.014;
H2O       18.015;
CO2       44.01;
```

This way, when the solver looks up `CH4`, it finds it directly in the `physicalProperties` dictionary rather than trying to look inside a `specie` sub-dictionary.

### Summary

The root issue across all attempts is that OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` expects species molecular weights to be defined as **flat top-level entries** in `physicalProperties`, not wrapped inside a `specie { }` sub-dictionary. Previous attempts added a `species` list and kept the `specie` block, but the solver needs the raw species data (molecular weights) to be present as dictionary keys at the top level of `physicalProperties`.</review_analysis>

<reviewer_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means that OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` expects a `specie` sub-dictionary **inside** the `physicalProperties` file for each species, rather than having a single shared `specie` block at the top level. Looking at the history of attempts:

- **Attempt 1**: Fixed `reactingMixture` → `multiComponentMixture` (correct)
- **Attempt 2**: Added `species` list (correct) but the error changed to `keyword species is undefined` → then fixed that
- Now: The error is `keyword CH4 is undefined` — meaning the individual species' `specie` sub-dictionaries are missing from `physicalProperties`

In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, each species must have its own `specie` entry **directly in** `physicalProperties` (not just one global `specie` block). The solver looks up `specie` for each species individually, and since there's only one global `specie` block containing all five species, it fails to find the individual `specie` entry for `CH4`.

## Proposed Solution

**Modify `constant/physicalProperties`** to include individual `specie` sub-dictionaries for each species directly in the main dictionary (or accessible from it). There are two approaches:

### Approach A (Preferred): Individual specie entries for each species

Place each species' `specie` sub-dictionary directly in `physicalProperties` at the top level, e.g.:

```
specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
}
specie
{
    O2        31.998;
}
specie
{
    N2        28.014;
}
specie
{
    H2O       18.015;
}
specie
{
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### Approach B: Single combined specie block with all entries

Alternatively, keep a single `specie` block but ensure it contains **all five species** with their full molecular weight data (which it already does). Wait — looking at the current file, the `specie` block **already contains all five species**. So why is it failing?

Actually, re-reading the error carefully: it says `keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary "...physicalProperties"`. This suggests the solver is looking for a **top-level entry named exactly `CH4`**, not finding it in the `specie` sub-dictionary. 

In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the solver expects each species to have its thermodynamic data available at the top level of `physicalProperties`. The correct format is to have entries like:

```
CH4       12.011;
O2        31.998;
N2        28.014;
H2O       18.015;
CO2       44.01;
```

...as **flat entries** in `physicalProperties`, not nested inside a `specie { }` block.

### Fix

Remove the `specie { ... }` block from `physicalProperties` entirely, and place the five species with their molecular weights as **direct top-level entries**:

```
inertSpecie N2;

CH4       12.011;
O2        31.998;
N2        28.014;
H2O       18.015;
CO2       44.01;
```

This way, when the solver looks up `CH4`, it finds it directly in the `physicalProperties` dictionary rather than trying to look inside a `specie` sub-dictionary.

### Summary

The root issue across all attempts is that OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` expects species molecular weights to be defined as **flat top-level entries** in `physicalProperties`, not wrapped inside a `specie { }` sub-dictionary. Previous attempts added a `species` list and kept the `specie` block, but the solver needs the raw species data (molecular weights) to be present as dictionary keys at the top level of `physicalProperties`.</reviewer_analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how thermophysical properties and chemistry are handled. The supported mixture types are `pureMixture`, `coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture`, `multiComponentMixture`, and `valueMultiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, OpenFOAM 10 has moved away from the old `hePsiThermo`/`chemicalSystem` framework toward a more unified `fluidReactionThermo` approach.

## Root Cause

The `physicalProperties` file specifies:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But OpenFOAM 10 does not recognize `reactingMixture`. Furthermore, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also uses `reactingMixture` and references `foamChemistryReader`, which is also outdated for OF10.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Change `mixture` type in `constant/physicalProperties`

In the `physicalProperties` file, change:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```
to:
```
mixture         multiComponentMixture;
```

Also update the `thermoType` block to remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries since those are not needed when using `multiComponentMixture` with species listed directly in the file. The `physicalProperties` should look like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### 2. Update `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Similarly, update `thermo.compressibleGas` to use `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

Remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries, and add the same `specie` sub-dictionary with molecular weights as shown above.

### 3. Verify `reactions` file compatibility

The `reactions` file should remain largely unchanged, but ensure the reaction is properly formatted. In OpenFOAM 10, the reaction format may need slight adjustment. Consider adding a `#include` directive or ensuring the reaction syntax follows the current format. Also verify that the `chemistryProperties` file's `chemistryType` settings are compatible — the `ode` solver with `standard` method should work fine.

### Summary

The primary fix is replacing `reactingMixture` with `multiComponentMixture` throughout the thermophysical property files (`physicalProperties` and `thermo.compressibleGas`), while removing the deprecated `chemistryReader`/`foamChemistry*` entries that are no longer used in OpenFOAM 10's fluid reaction thermo framework. This aligns with the OpenFOAM 10-supported mixture types and resolves the compilation error.

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This means that OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` expects a `specie` sub-dictionary **inside** the `physicalProperties` file for each species, rather than having a single shared `specie` block at the top level. Looking at the history of attempts:

- **Attempt 1**: Fixed `reactingMixture` → `multiComponentMixture` (correct)
- **Attempt 2**: Added `species` list (correct) but the error changed to `keyword species is undefined` → then fixed that
- Now: The error is `keyword CH4 is undefined` — meaning the individual species' `specie` sub-dictionaries are missing from `physicalProperties`

In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, each species must have its own `specie` entry **directly in** `physicalProperties` (not just one global `specie` block). The solver looks up `specie` for each species individually, and since there's only one global `specie` block containing all five species, it fails to find the individual `specie` entry for `CH4`.

## Proposed Solution

**Modify `constant/physicalProperties`** to include individual `specie` sub-dictionaries for each species directly in the main dictionary (or accessible from it). There are two approaches:

### Approach A (Preferred): Individual specie entries for each species

Place each species' `specie` sub-dictionary directly in `physicalProperties` at the top level, e.g.:

```
specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
}
specie
{
    O2        31.998;
}
specie
{
    N2        28.014;
}
specie
{
    H2O       18.015;
}
specie
{
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### Approach B: Single combined specie block with all entries

Alternatively, keep a single `specie` block but ensure it contains **all five species** with their full molecular weight data (which it already does). Wait — looking at the current file, the `specie` block **already contains all five species**. So why is it failing?

Actually, re-reading the error carefully: it says `keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary "...physicalProperties"`. This suggests the solver is looking for a **top-level entry named exactly `CH4`**, not finding it in the `specie` sub-dictionary. 

In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the solver expects each species to have its thermodynamic data available at the top level of `physicalProperties`. The correct format is to have entries like:

```
CH4       12.011;
O2        31.998;
N2        28.014;
H2O       18.015;
CO2       44.01;
```

...as **flat entries** in `physicalProperties`, not nested inside a `specie { }` block.

### Fix

Remove the `specie { ... }` block from `physicalProperties` entirely, and place the five species with their molecular weights as **direct top-level entries**:

```
inertSpecie N2;

CH4       12.011;
O2        31.998;
N2        28.014;
H2O       18.015;
CO2       44.01;
```

This way, when the solver looks up `CH4`, it finds it directly in the `physicalProperties` dictionary rather than trying to look inside a `specie` sub-dictionary.

### Summary

The root issue across all attempts is that OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` expects species molecular weights to be defined as **flat top-level entries** in `physicalProperties`, not wrapped inside a `specie { }` sub-dictionary. Previous attempts added a `species` list and kept the `specie` block, but the solver needs the raw species data (molecular weights) to be present as dictionary keys at the top level of `physicalProperties`.

<review_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This is the same error as Attempt 2, meaning the `species` list was added but the solver still can't find it. Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file provided, I can see it **does** contain the `species` entry:

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);
```

However, OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` with `hePsiThermo` actually requires the `species` list to be inside a `specie` sub-dictionary (or the solver has changed expectations between attempts). But wait — looking more carefully at the history, the error keeps changing form because each fix spawns a new error.

Let me re-examine what OpenFOAM 10 actually wants. In OpenFOAM 10's `fluidReactionThermo` framework with `multiComponentMixture`, the `species` list should indeed be at the top level of `physicalProperties`. Since it's already there and the error persists, the real issue might be that the `thermo.compressibleGas` file still contains problematic entries.

## Root Cause

The `thermo.compressibleGas` file still contains the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries. These are deprecated in OpenFOAM 10 and may interfere with the direct-species-in-physical-properties approach. They could be causing the `physicalProperties` file to not be parsed correctly overall.

Additionally, looking at the `reactions` file, the reaction is defined as `irreversibleArrheniusReaction`, but in OpenFOAM 10 the reaction type naming may have changed.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Simplify `constant/physicalProperties` — Ensure `species` is at top level

The `species` list is already present at the top level, so this should be sufficient. However, make sure there are no syntax issues. Keep it as-is for now.

### 2. Clean up `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` completely

Remove the following entries entirely since they are deprecated in OpenFOAM 10 and conflict with the direct species approach in `physicalProperties`:

- Delete the `chemistryReader` block
- Delete the `foamChemistryFile` entry
- Delete the `foamChemistryThermoFile` entry

The `thermo.compressibleGas` should only contain the `thermoType` block with `multiComponentMixture` (and the `specie` sub-dictionary if desired).

### 3. Verify `reactions` file format compatibility

In OpenFOAM 10, the reaction type may need to be `arrheniusReaction` instead of `irreversibleArrheniusReaction`, or the format may require additional fields. Check against the tutorial and update if needed. Also ensure the `chemistryProperties` file is compatible — the `odeCoeffs` section may need attention.

### 4. Verify `chemistryProperties` format

Ensure the `chemistryType` and `odeCoeffs` sections are compatible with OpenFOAM 10. The current `ode` solver with `seulex` coefficients should be fine, but double-check the absolute tolerance values are appropriate.

### Summary

The primary action is **cleaning up `thermo.compressibleGas`** by removing the three deprecated entries (`chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, `foamChemistryThermoFile`). These entries are remnants of older OpenFOAM versions and are not needed (and can cause harm) when species are defined directly in `physicalProperties` with `multiComponentMixture`. This has been the persistent source of parsing issues across all attempts.</review_analysis>

<reviewer_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This is the same error as Attempt 2, meaning the `species` list was added but the solver still can't find it. Looking at the current `physicalProperties` file provided, I can see it **does** contain the `species` entry:

```
species
(
    CH4
    O2
    N2
    H2O
    CO2
);
```

However, OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` with `hePsiThermo` actually requires the `species` list to be inside a `specie` sub-dictionary (or the solver has changed expectations between attempts). But wait — looking more carefully at the history, the error keeps changing form because each fix spawns a new error.

Let me re-examine what OpenFOAM 10 actually wants. In OpenFOAM 10's `fluidReactionThermo` framework with `multiComponentMixture`, the `species` list should indeed be at the top level of `physicalProperties`. Since it's already there and the error persists, the real issue might be that the `thermo.compressibleGas` file still contains problematic entries.

## Root Cause

The `thermo.compressibleGas` file still contains the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries. These are deprecated in OpenFOAM 10 and may interfere with the direct-species-in-physical-properties approach. They could be causing the `physicalProperties` file to not be parsed correctly overall.

Additionally, looking at the `reactions` file, the reaction is defined as `irreversibleArrheniusReaction`, but in OpenFOAM 10 the reaction type naming may have changed.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Simplify `constant/physicalProperties` — Ensure `species` is at top level

The `species` list is already present at the top level, so this should be sufficient. However, make sure there are no syntax issues. Keep it as-is for now.

### 2. Clean up `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` completely

Remove the following entries entirely since they are deprecated in OpenFOAM 10 and conflict with the direct species approach in `physicalProperties`:

- Delete the `chemistryReader` block
- Delete the `foamChemistryFile` entry
- Delete the `foamChemistryThermoFile` entry

The `thermo.compressibleGas` should only contain the `thermoType` block with `multiComponentMixture` (and the `specie` sub-dictionary if desired).

### 3. Verify `reactions` file format compatibility

In OpenFOAM 10, the reaction type may need to be `arrheniusReaction` instead of `irreversibleArrheniusReaction`, or the format may require additional fields. Check against the tutorial and update if needed. Also ensure the `chemistryProperties` file is compatible — the `odeCoeffs` section may need attention.

### 4. Verify `chemistryProperties` format

Ensure the `chemistryType` and `odeCoeffs` sections are compatible with OpenFOAM 10. The current `ode` solver with `seulex` coefficients should be fine, but double-check the absolute tolerance values are appropriate.

### Summary

The primary action is **cleaning up `thermo.compressibleGas`** by removing the three deprecated entries (`chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, `foamChemistryThermoFile`). These entries are remnants of older OpenFOAM versions and are not needed (and can cause harm) when species are defined directly in `physicalProperties` with `multiComponentMixture`. This has been the persistent source of parsing issues across all attempts.</reviewer_analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how thermophysical properties and chemistry are handled. The supported mixture types are `pureMixture`, `coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture`, `multiComponentMixture`, and `valueMultiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, OpenFOAM 10 has moved away from the old `hePsiThermo`/`chemicalSystem` framework toward a more unified `fluidReactionThermo` approach.

## Root Cause

The `physicalProperties` file specifies:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But OpenFOAM 10 does not recognize `reactingMixture`. Furthermore, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also uses `reactingMixture` and references `foamChemistryReader`, which is also outdated for OF10.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Change `mixture` type in `constant/physicalProperties`

In the `physicalProperties` file, change:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```
to:
```
mixture         multiComponentMixture;
```

Also update the `thermoType` block to remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries since those are not needed when using `multiComponentMixture` with species listed directly in the file. The `physicalProperties` should look like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### 2. Update `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Similarly, update `thermo.compressibleGas` to use `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

Remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries, and add the same `specie` sub-dictionary with molecular weights as shown above.

### 3. Verify `reactions` file compatibility

The `reactions` file should remain largely unchanged, but ensure the reaction is properly formatted. In OpenFOAM 10, the reaction format may need slight adjustment. Consider adding a `#include` directive or ensuring the reaction syntax follows the current format. Also verify that the `chemistryProperties` file's `chemistryType` settings are compatible — the `ode` solver with `standard` method should work fine.

### Summary

The primary fix is replacing `reactingMixture` with `multiComponentMixture` throughout the thermophysical property files (`physicalProperties` and `thermo.compressibleGas`), while removing the deprecated `chemistryReader`/`foamChemistry*` entries that are no longer used in OpenFOAM 10's fluid reaction thermo framework. This aligns with the OpenFOAM 10-supported mixture types and resolves the compilation error.

- Now: The error is `keyword CH4 is undefined` — meaning the individual species' `specie` sub-dictionaries are missing from `physicalProperties`

In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, each species must have its own `specie` entry **directly in** `physicalProperties` (not just one global `specie` block). The solver looks up `specie` for each species individually, and since there's only one global `specie` block containing all five species, it fails to find the individual `specie` entry for `CH4`.

## Proposed Solution

**Modify `constant/physicalProperties`** to include individual `specie` sub-dictionaries for each species directly in the main dictionary (or accessible from it). There are two approaches:

### Approach A (Preferred): Individual specie entries for each species

Place each species' `specie` sub-dictionary directly in `physicalProperties` at the top level, e.g.:

```

{
    CH4       12.011;
}
specie
{
    O2        31.998;
}
specie
{
    N2        28.014;
}
specie
{
    H2O       18.015;
}
specie
{
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### Approach B: Single combined specie block with all entries

Alternatively, keep a single `specie` block but ensure it contains **all five species** with their full molecular weight data (which it already does). Wait — looking at the current file, the `specie` block **already contains all five species**. So why is it failing?

Actually, re-reading the error carefully: it says `keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary "...physicalProperties"`. This suggests the solver is looking for a **top-level entry named exactly `CH4`**, not finding it in the `specie` sub-dictionary. 

In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the solver expects each species to have its thermodynamic data available at the top level of `physicalProperties`. The correct format is to have entries like:

```
CH4       12.011;
O2        31.998;
N2        28.014;
H2O       18.015;
CO2       44.01;
```

...as **flat entries** in `physicalProperties`, not nested inside a `specie { }` block.

### Fix

Remove the `specie { ... }` block from `physicalProperties` entirely, and place the five species with their molecular weights as **direct top-level entries**:

```
inertSpecie N2;

CH4       12.011;
O2        31.998;
N2        28.014;
H2O       18.015;
CO2       44.01;
```

This way, when the solver looks up `CH4`, it finds it directly in the `physicalProperties` dictionary rather than trying to look inside a `specie` sub-dictionary.

### Summary

The root issue across all attempts is that OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` expects species molecular weights to be defined as **flat top-level entries** in `physicalProperties`, not wrapped inside a `specie { }` sub-dictionary. Previous attempts added a `species` list and kept the `specie` block, but the solver needs the raw species data (molecular weights) to be present as dictionary keys at the top level of `physicalProperties`.

<review_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This is the same error as Attempts 2 and 4. The `species` list IS present in `physicalProperties`, yet OpenFOAM 10 still cannot find it. This suggests that in OpenFOAM 10's `fluidReactionThermo` framework with `multiComponentMixture`, the solver is looking for the `species` keyword in a different location or format than expected.

After reviewing the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial case structure referenced in the similar case, I notice that in OpenFOAM 10, the `physicalProperties` file needs to follow the exact tutorial convention. The tutorial uses a specific format where the species thermodynamic data is organized differently.

## Root Cause

Looking at the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial in OpenFOAM 10, the `physicalProperties` file with `multiComponentMixture` expects the species molecular weights to be defined **inside a `specie` sub-dictionary** at the top level, NOT as flat entries. The tutorial format is:

```
specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

Instead of flat entries. And critically, the `species` list should either be omitted entirely or it should be inside the same `specie` block context. Actually, in OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the solver reads the species list from within the `specie` sub-dictionary context.

Furthermore, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file likely still contains problematic entries from previous attempts that are interfering.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Rewrite `constant/physicalProperties` to match the tutorial format exactly

Remove the flat `CH4 12.011;` style entries and wrap them inside a proper `specie` sub-dictionary at the top level:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

Note: Remove the explicit `species (...)` list entirely. In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the solver derives the species list from the `specie` sub-dictionary keys. Having both a separate `species` list AND a `specie` block can cause confusion.

### 2. Ensure `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` is clean

The `thermo.compressibleGas` file should have a minimal `thermoType` block with `multiComponentMixture` and the same `specie` sub-dictionary format as above. Remove any `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, or `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries if they exist.

### 3. Verify `reactions` file compatibility

Ensure the reaction is defined as a simple `arrheniusReaction` (without the `irreversible` prefix) since OpenFOAM 10 may not support `irreversibleArrheniusReaction`. The reaction format `"CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O"` should be fine.

### Summary

The core fix is restructuring `physicalProperties` to use the tutorial-compliant format: a single `specie` sub-dictionary at the top level containing all species with their molecular weights, and **remove the separate `species` list**. In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the species list is derived from the `specie` dictionary keys, so having both creates ambiguity. The `specie` sub-dictionary must be present (referenced by `specie specie;`) and should contain all five species' molecular weight data directly inside it.</review_analysis>

<reviewer_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This is the same error as Attempts 2 and 4. The `species` list IS present in `physicalProperties`, yet OpenFOAM 10 still cannot find it. This suggests that in OpenFOAM 10's `fluidReactionThermo` framework with `multiComponentMixture`, the solver is looking for the `species` keyword in a different location or format than expected.

After reviewing the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial case structure referenced in the similar case, I notice that in OpenFOAM 10, the `physicalProperties` file needs to follow the exact tutorial convention. The tutorial uses a specific format where the species thermodynamic data is organized differently.

## Root Cause

Looking at the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial in OpenFOAM 10, the `physicalProperties` file with `multiComponentMixture` expects the species molecular weights to be defined **inside a `specie` sub-dictionary** at the top level, NOT as flat entries. The tutorial format is:

```
specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

Instead of flat entries. And critically, the `species` list should either be omitted entirely or it should be inside the same `specie` block context. Actually, in OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the solver reads the species list from within the `specie` sub-dictionary context.

Furthermore, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file likely still contains problematic entries from previous attempts that are interfering.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Rewrite `constant/physicalProperties` to match the tutorial format exactly

Remove the flat `CH4 12.011;` style entries and wrap them inside a proper `specie` sub-dictionary at the top level:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

Note: Remove the explicit `species (...)` list entirely. In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the solver derives the species list from the `specie` sub-dictionary keys. Having both a separate `species` list AND a `specie` block can cause confusion.

### 2. Ensure `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` is clean

The `thermo.compressibleGas` file should have a minimal `thermoType` block with `multiComponentMixture` and the same `specie` sub-dictionary format as above. Remove any `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, or `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries if they exist.

### 3. Verify `reactions` file compatibility

Ensure the reaction is defined as a simple `arrheniusReaction` (without the `irreversible` prefix) since OpenFOAM 10 may not support `irreversibleArrheniusReaction`. The reaction format `"CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O"` should be fine.

### Summary

The core fix is restructuring `physicalProperties` to use the tutorial-compliant format: a single `specie` sub-dictionary at the top level containing all species with their molecular weights, and **remove the separate `species` list**. In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the species list is derived from the `specie` dictionary keys, so having both creates ambiguity. The `specie` sub-dictionary must be present (referenced by `specie specie;`) and should contain all five species' molecular weight data directly inside it.</reviewer_analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how thermophysical properties and chemistry are handled. The supported mixture types are `pureMixture`, `coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture`, `multiComponentMixture`, and `valueMultiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, OpenFOAM 10 has moved away from the old `hePsiThermo`/`chemicalSystem` framework toward a more unified `fluidReactionThermo` approach.

## Root Cause

The `physicalProperties` file specifies:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But OpenFOAM 10 does not recognize `reactingMixture`. Furthermore, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also uses `reactingMixture` and references `foamChemistryReader`, which is also outdated for OF10.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Change `mixture` type in `constant/physicalProperties`

In the `physicalProperties` file, change:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```
to:
```
mixture         multiComponentMixture;
```

Also update the `thermoType` block to remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries since those are not needed when using `multiComponentMixture` with species listed directly in the file. The `physicalProperties` should look like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### 2. Update `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Similarly, update `thermo.compressibleGas` to use `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

Remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries, and add the same `specie` sub-dictionary with molecular weights as shown above.

### 3. Verify `reactions` file compatibility

The `reactions` file should remain largely unchanged, but ensure the reaction is properly formatted. In OpenFOAM 10, the reaction format may need slight adjustment. Consider adding a `#include` directive or ensuring the reaction syntax follows the current format. Also verify that the `chemistryProperties` file's `chemistryType` settings are compatible — the `ode` solver with `standard` method should work fine.

### Summary

The primary fix is replacing `reactingMixture` with `multiComponentMixture` throughout the thermophysical property files (`physicalProperties` and `thermo.compressibleGas`), while removing the deprecated `chemistryReader`/`foamChemistry*` entries that are no longer used in OpenFOAM 10's fluid reaction thermo framework. This aligns with the OpenFOAM 10-supported mixture types and resolves the compilation error.

In OpenFOAM 10 with `multiComponentMixture`, each species must have its own `specie` entry **directly in** `physicalProperties` (not just one global `specie` block). The solver looks up `specie` for each species individually, and since there's only one global `specie` block containing all five species, it fails to find the individual `specie` entry for `CH4`.

## Proposed Solution

**Modify `constant/physicalProperties`** to include individual `specie` sub-dictionaries for each species directly in the main dictionary (or accessible from it). There are two approaches:

### Approach A (Preferred): Individual specie entries for each species

Place each species' `specie` sub-dictionary directly in `physicalProperties` at the top level, e.g.:

```

    CH4       12.011;
}
specie
{

}
specie
{

}
specie
{

specie

}

### Approach B: Single combined specie block with all entries

Alternatively, keep a single `specie` block but ensure it contains **all five species** with their full molecular weight data (which it already does). Wait — looking at the current file, the `specie` block **already contains all five species**. So why is it failing?

Actually, re-reading the error carefully: it says `keyword CH4 is undefined in dictionary "...physicalProperties"`. This suggests the solver is looking for a **top-level entry named exactly `CH4`**, not finding it in the `specie` sub-dictionary. 

In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the solver expects each species to have its thermodynamic data available at the top level of `physicalProperties`. The correct format is to have entries like:

```
CH4       12.011;
O2        31.998;
N2        28.014;
H2O       18.015;
CO2       44.01;
```

...as **flat entries** in `physicalProperties`, not nested inside a `specie { }` block.

### Fix

Remove the `specie { ... }` block from `physicalProperties` entirely, and place the five species with their molecular weights as **direct top-level entries**:

```

CH4       12.011;
O2        31.998;
N2        28.014;
H2O       18.015;
CO2       44.01;
```

This way, when the solver looks up `CH4`, it finds it directly in the `physicalProperties` dictionary rather than trying to look inside a `specie` sub-dictionary.

### Summary

The root issue across all attempts is that OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` expects species molecular weights to be defined as **flat top-level entries** in `physicalProperties`, not wrapped inside a `specie { }` sub-dictionary. Previous attempts added a `species` list and kept the `specie` block, but the solver needs the raw species data (molecular weights) to be present as dictionary keys at the top level of `physicalProperties`.

The tutorial uses a specific format where the species thermodynamic data is organized differently.

## Root Cause

Looking at the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial in OpenFOAM 10, the `physicalProperties` file with `multiComponentMixture` expects the species molecular weights to be defined **inside a `specie` sub-dictionary** at the top level, NOT as flat entries. The tutorial format is:

```
specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

Instead of flat entries. And critically, the `species` list should either be omitted entirely or it should be inside the same `specie` block context. Actually, in OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the solver reads the species list from within the `specie` sub-dictionary context.

Furthermore, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file likely still contains problematic entries from previous attempts that are interfering.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Rewrite `constant/physicalProperties` to match the tutorial format exactly

Remove the flat `CH4 12.011;` style entries and wrap them inside a proper `specie` sub-dictionary at the top level:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

Note: Remove the explicit `species (...)` list entirely. In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the solver derives the species list from the `specie` sub-dictionary keys. Having both a separate `species` list AND a `specie` block can cause confusion.

### 2. Ensure `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` is clean

The `thermo.compressibleGas` file should have a minimal `thermoType` block with `multiComponentMixture` and the same `specie` sub-dictionary format as above. Remove any `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, or `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries if they exist.

### 3. Verify `reactions` file compatibility

Ensure the reaction is defined as a simple `arrheniusReaction` (without the `irreversible` prefix) since OpenFOAM 10 may not support `irreversibleArrheniusReaction`. The reaction format `"CH4 + 2O2 = CO2 + 2H2O"` should be fine.

### Summary

The core fix is restructuring `physicalProperties` to use the tutorial-compliant format: a single `specie` sub-dictionary at the top level containing all species with their molecular weights, and **remove the separate `species` list**. In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the species list is derived from the `specie` dictionary keys, so having both creates ambiguity. <review_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This is the same error persisting across multiple attempts (2, 4, 5). Despite adding a `species` list, defining `specie` sub-dictionary, and various combinations, OpenFOAM 10 continues to fail to find the `species` keyword.

Looking at the current state of `physicalProperties` provided in the foamfiles list, I can see it currently contains:

- `thermoType` with `multiComponentMixture`
- `inertSpecie N2;`
- A `species` list with all five species
- A `specie` sub-dictionary with all five species' molecular weights

Yet the error persists. This tells me that in **OpenFOAM 10**, the `multiComponentMixture` type with `hePsiThermo` actually expects the `species` list to be inside the `specie` sub-dictionary itself, or the lookup mechanism works differently.

## New Approach — Based on the counterFlowFlame2D Tutorial Structure

Looking at the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial case reference and considering that OpenFOAM 10 has restructured how thermophysical properties work, I believe the issue is that `multiComponentMixture` in OF10 expects the species information to be structured such that the solver can find a `species` entry **within the `specie` sub-dictionary context**.

However, the real breakthrough insight comes from examining the `thermo.compressibleGas` file. In OpenFOAM 10, the `fluidReactionThermo` framework may require that the `thermo.compressibleGas` file contains a `chemistryReader` pointing to a valid chemistry file, OR the species list must be extracted from the reactions context.

But the most likely scenario based on the persistent error is that OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` with `hePsiThermo` expects the `species` list to be present **and the solver needs to be able to parse it**. The fact that it's been failing consistently suggests the parser is looking for something very specific.

## Proposed Solution

### 1. Restructure `constant/physicalProperties` — Place `species` INSIDE the `specie` sub-dictionary

In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the `species` list should be nested **inside** the `specie` sub-dictionary, not as a separate top-level entry. Remove the standalone `species (...)` list and embed it within the `specie` block:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    species
    (
        CH4
        O2
        N2
        H2O
        CO2
    );

    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

This nests the `species` list inside the `specie` sub-dictionary, which is where OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` expects to find it when using `hePsiThermo`.

### 2. Ensure `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` is clean

The `thermo.compressibleGas` file should have a minimal `thermoType` block with `multiComponentMixture` and the same `specie` sub-dictionary format (with nested `species` list). No `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, or `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries.

### Summary

The key difference from previous attempts is that in OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` with `hePsiThermo`, the `species` list must be **nested inside** the `specie` sub-dictionary rather than being a separate top-level entry. The solver searches for `species` within the context of the `specie` dictionary. This explains why all prior attempts failed despite having a `species` list at the top level — the lookup mechanism looks inside `specie` first.</review_analysis>

<reviewer_analysis># Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is:

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
```

This is the same error persisting across multiple attempts (2, 4, 5). Despite adding a `species` list, defining `specie` sub-dictionary, and various combinations, OpenFOAM 10 continues to fail to find the `species` keyword.

Looking at the current state of `physicalProperties` provided in the foamfiles list, I can see it currently contains:

- `thermoType` with `multiComponentMixture`
- `inertSpecie N2;`
- A `species` list with all five species
- A `specie` sub-dictionary with all five species' molecular weights

Yet the error persists. This tells me that in **OpenFOAM 10**, the `multiComponentMixture` type with `hePsiThermo` actually expects the `species` list to be inside the `specie` sub-dictionary itself, or the lookup mechanism works differently.

## New Approach — Based on the counterFlowFlame2D Tutorial Structure

Looking at the counterFlowFlame2D tutorial case reference and considering that OpenFOAM 10 has restructured how thermophysical properties work, I believe the issue is that `multiComponentMixture` in OF10 expects the species information to be structured such that the solver can find a `species` entry **within the `specie` sub-dictionary context**.

However, the real breakthrough insight comes from examining the `thermo.compressibleGas` file. In OpenFOAM 10, the `fluidReactionThermo` framework may require that the `thermo.compressibleGas` file contains a `chemistryReader` pointing to a valid chemistry file, OR the species list must be extracted from the reactions context.

But the most likely scenario based on the persistent error is that OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` with `hePsiThermo` expects the `species` list to be present **and the solver needs to be able to parse it**. The fact that it's been failing consistently suggests the parser is looking for something very specific.

## Proposed Solution

### 1. Restructure `constant/physicalProperties` — Place `species` INSIDE the `specie` sub-dictionary

In OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture`, the `species` list should be nested **inside** the `specie` sub-dictionary, not as a separate top-level entry. Remove the standalone `species (...)` list and embed it within the `specie` block:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    species
    (
        CH4
        O2
        N2
        H2O
        CO2
    );

    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

This nests the `species` list inside the `specie` sub-dictionary, which is where OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` expects to find it when using `hePsiThermo`.

### 2. Ensure `constant/thermo.compressibleGas` is clean

The `thermo.compressibleGas` file should have a minimal `thermoType` block with `multiComponentMixture` and the same `specie` sub-dictionary format (with nested `species` list). No `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, or `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries.

### Summary

The key difference from previous attempts is that in OpenFOAM 10's `multiComponentMixture` with `hePsiThermo`, the `species` list must be **nested inside** the `specie` sub-dictionary rather than being a separate top-level entry. The solver searches for `species` within the context of the `specie` dictionary. This explains why all prior attempts failed despite having a `species` list at the top level — the lookup mechanism looks inside `specie` first.</reviewer_analysis>

# Error Diagnosis and Resolution

## Error Analysis

The error message is clear:

```
Unknown mixture type reactingMixture
Supported mixture types:
4
(
pureMixture
coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture
multiComponentMixture
valueMultiComponentMixture
)
```

This indicates that in **OpenFOAM 10** (as seen from the path `/global/cfs/cdirs/m4756/theseus/OpenFOAM-10/`), the mixture type `reactingMixture` is **no longer supported**. The Foundation version (OpenFOAM 10) restructured how thermophysical properties and chemistry are handled. The supported mixture types are `pureMixture`, `coefficientWilkeMultiComponentMixture`, `multiComponentMixture`, and `valueMultiComponentMixture`.

Additionally, OpenFOAM 10 has moved away from the old `hePsiThermo`/`chemicalSystem` framework toward a more unified `fluidReactionThermo` approach.

## Root Cause

The `physicalProperties` file specifies:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```

But OpenFOAM 10 does not recognize `reactingMixture`. Furthermore, the `thermo.compressibleGas` file also uses `reactingMixture` and references `foamChemistryReader`, which is also outdated for OF10.

## Proposed Solutions

### 1. Change `mixture` type in `constant/physicalProperties`

In the `physicalProperties` file, change:
```
mixture         reactingMixture;
```
to:
```
mixture         multiComponentMixture;
```

Also update the `thermoType` block to remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries since those are not needed when using `multiComponentMixture` with species listed directly in the file. The `physicalProperties` should look like:

```
thermoType
{
    type            hePsiThermo;
    mixture         multiComponentMixture;
    transport       sutherland;
    thermo          janaf;
    energy          sensibleEnthalpy;
    equationOfState perfectGas;
    specie          specie;
}

inertSpecie N2;

specie
{
    CH4       12.011;
    O2        31.998;
    N2        28.014;
    H2O       18.015;
    CO2       44.01;
}
```

### 2. Update `constant/thermo.compressibleGas`

Similarly, update `thermo.compressibleGas` to use `multiComponentMixture` instead of `reactingMixture`:

Remove the `chemistryReader`, `foamChemistryFile`, and `foamChemistryThermoFile` entries, and add the same `specie` sub-dictionary with molecular weights as shown above.

### 3. Verify `reactions` file compatibility

The `reactions` file should remain largely unchanged, but ensure the reaction is properly formatted. In OpenFOAM 10, the reaction format may need slight adjustment. Consider adding a `#include` directive or ensuring the reaction syntax follows the current format. Also verify that the `chemistryProperties` file's `chemistryType` settings are compatible — the `ode` solver with `standard` method should work fine.

### Summary

The primary fix is replacing `reactingMixture` with `multiComponentMixture` throughout the thermophysical property files (`physicalProperties` and `thermo.compressibleGas`), while removing the deprecated `chemistryReader`/`foamChemistry*` entries that are no longer used in OpenFOAM 10's fluid reaction thermo framework. This aligns with the OpenFOAM 10-supported mixture types and resolves the compilation error.

```
keyword species is undefined in dictionary "constant/physicalProperties"
